items.q: In a fair test, how many variables should you change at once?
items.correct: Only one, so any difference in results can be linked to that change.
items.misconception: As many as possible to get faster results.
items.random_wrong: None, because variables should never change.
items.why: Students change several variables together, making it impossible to know which caused the effect.

items.q: What is the purpose of a control in a food investigation?
items.correct: To provide a standard, unchanged sample to compare the test results against.
items.misconception: To make the experiment more complicated.
items.random_wrong: To increase the cost of the project.
items.why: Learners overlook that a control gives a baseline for valid comparison.

items.q: Does sensory testing give purely objective results?
items.correct: It is made more reliable with panels and structured tests, but tasting still involves personal judgement.
items.misconception: Sensory scores are completely objective like a thermometer reading.
items.random_wrong: Sensory testing measures the calories in food.
items.why: Students assume sensory data is fully objective, ignoring the human element that panels aim to reduce.

items.q: In a fair test comparing two recipes, you should change:
items.correct: Only one variable at a time
items.why: Correct answer: Only one variable at a time. “Every variable at once” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A spider diagram showing scores for taste, texture and aroma is called a:
items.why: It's Star profile. “Bar chart” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A clear, testable prediction that an investigation sets out to examine is a:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Hypothesis. “Conclusion” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Using a tasting panel with ranking tests to compare products is an example of:
items.why: Correct answer: Sensory evaluation. “Nutritional analysis” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
